text: The surgical team has placed the patient in the French position and inserted five trocars for the laparoscopic total gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic gastrectomy by dissecting the omentum at its colic insertion, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the omental tissue from the colon.

The surgeon carefully navigates the adhesions between the omentum and the mesocolon in a patient with significant fatty tissue, switching from active sealing to blunt dissection to identify the correct tissue plane and prevent injury to the mesocolon.
After complete dissection of the omentum from its colic insertion, the posterior gastric wall is exposed, revealing visible retractions caused by the tumor.
The surgeon continues the dissection from left to right, exposing the head of the pancreas and the station six lymph node. The right gastroepiploic vein and artery are identified for control.
The surgeon uses traction on the pylorus to aid in identifying the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, which can be challenging to locate in patients with significant adipose tissue.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection with a suction device to identify the upper limit of the head of the pancreas, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to perform a precise dissection of the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, carefully navigating around these vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon controls the early division of the right gastroepiploic artery, ensuring precise management of this vessel during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection at station six, controlling the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, and places a gauze posterior to the pylorus for hemostasis and exposure.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection, exposing station five. After complete dissection of the pylorus, a twelve millimeter trocar is inserted for stapling the first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the territory of the right gastric artery, controlling a small branch that extends directly to the duodenum. The procedure then advances to the dissection of the hepatic hilum.
The surgeon carefully dissects around lymph node station twelve, located alongside the hepatic artery, using precise techniques to separate the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the early division of the right gastric artery, applying clips to control lymphatic vessels to prevent lymphatic leakage.

The surgeon carefully dissects alongside the portal vein using a coagulating hook, focusing on lymph node station five near the right gastric artery. The dissection continues towards station eight along the hepatic artery.

The surgeon performs an upper dissection of the lesser omentum, exposing lymph node station number one. The dissection continues around the left gastric artery and vein, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs a cephalic dissection, addressing lymph node station seven and progressing towards lymph node station one. Subsequently, the dissection shifts to the upper left region of the stomach.
The surgeon carefully dissects the left gastroepiploic vein and artery, which are challenging to locate in fatigued patients, using precise techniques to navigate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissects the gastrosplenic ligament, taking care to avoid entering the plane of the splenic hilum to prevent major bleeding.

The surgeon applies major traction to the stomach towards the right side, exposing lymph node station two for completion of the lymph node dissection. The last few centimeters of the esophagus are freed from adhesions, preparing to expose the lower third of the esophagus for the stapling device.

The surgeon completes the dissection near the oesophagus, ensuring not to extend too deeply into the thorax due to the vascular supply from the aorta. The specimen is prepared for retrieval through a fan and steel incision.
The surgeon identifies the angle of Treitz and elevates the jejunum to prepare for the creation of an ozojejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a blue cartridge stapler to perform a linear lateral anastomosis, with the assistant's trocar stretching the limb on the stapler to ensure alignment. Two half running sutures with PDS 2-0 are then used to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon ties the two half running sutures together at the midpoint of the previous anterotomy. Subsequently, a 60-centimeter alimentary limb is measured, preparing for the next step in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a laterolateral jejuno-jejunal anastomosis using a linear stapler with a white cartridge. The anastomosis site is then closed with two half running sutures.

The surgeon divides the ozojejunal and jejunojejunal anastomosis. An aortic test is performed with the anesthesiologist's assistance, ensuring no air bubbles are present. The oestrogen anastomosis is then secured to the right crus using silk sutures.
The surgeon closes the 12mm trocar port and places a drainage tube in the surgical site.
